Details: According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the achievable air stripping removal efficiency for 1,2-Dichloroethylene (both cis- and trans- isomers) is more than 80 percent, to concentrations lower than 10 µg/L (WHO, 2011). A full-scale study of packed tower aeration (PTA) by Hand, et al. (1986) reported 96.9 percent removal of cis-1,2-Dichloroethylene, from an influent concentration of 82.3 µg/L (greater than the current MCL) to a treated concentration of 2.6 µg/L (96.8 percent removal) [2303]. A bench-scale study of PTA starting from a much higher concentration (9,270 µg/L) found greater than 99 percent removal [2687]. An EPA pilot study of diffuse aeration found greater than 80 percent removal and calculated cost of treatment for various scenarios [2688]. Henry’s Law constants reported in several sources are high, ranging from 0.0934 to 0.307 atm m3/m3, indicating good treatment feasibility (Hand et al., 1986; Staudinger and Roberts, 2001; U.S. EPA, 1995). EPA has categorized the strippability of cis-1,2-Dichloroethylene as good (56 FR 3526, January 30, 1991).
